reasoning: The base date of August 3 corresponds to the immediate aftermath of a severe Friday, August 1 jobs report (73k print, massive 258k negative revisions). This triggered a spike in the B-rated high yield from 6.96 to 7.04 on August 1 as spreads blew out on recession fears. Because major corporate credit selloffs typically take several days to peak (much like the early August 2024 panic which peaked a few days after the jobs report), there is a strong probability (0.62) that the yield will remain above 7.04 by August 10. However, for the medium and longer term resolution dates, the probability of the yield being higher decreases. The Federal Reserve is now highly likely to counter these recessionary concerns with aggressive rate cuts (which were already being pushed for by two dissenting governors in July). Unless a deep, protracted recession actually materializes—which would cause widespread defaults and blow spreads to 800+ bps—the base case is that the risk-free rate will fall significantly over the fall and winter, dragging the effective nominal yield of B-rated debt back below 7.04. Thus, probabilities for September, November, and January dip below 0.50, before trending toward 0.50 exactly at the furthest horizon to reflect maximum uncertainty between a soft landing and a severe recession.
